questions.question: State the Principle of Mathematical Induction. Explain the three components required for a proof by induction.
keyPoints: Principle: If P(1) is true and P(k)→P(k+1) is true for all k≥1, then P(n) is true for all n∈ℕ
Three components: Base Case, Inductive Hypothesis, Inductive Step
Base Case: Prove P(1) or starting value
Inductive Hypothesis: Assume P(k) is true for arbitrary k
Inductive Step: Prove P(k+1) using the hypothesis

questions.question: Differentiate between Weak (Simple) Induction and Strong Induction with an example when each is preferred.
keyPoints: Weak Induction: Assumes P(k) is true to prove P(k+1)
Strong Induction: Assumes P(1), P(2), ..., P(k) are all true to prove P(k+1)
Strong induction preferred when P(k+1) depends on multiple previous cases
Example: Proving every integer >1 has prime factorization - needs all previous numbers
Both are logically equivalent but one may be easier to apply

questions.question: Using mathematical induction, prove that 1² + 2² + 3² + ... + n² = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6 for all n∈ℕ.
keyPoints: Base Case: n=1, LHS=1²=1, RHS=1(2)(3)/6=1, True
Inductive Hypothesis: Assume true for n=k: 1²+...+k² = k(k+1)(2k+1)/6
Inductive Step: Add (k+1)² to LHS
Simplify to get (k+1)(k+2)(2k+3)/6, which equals formula for n=k+1
Therefore true for all n by induction

questions.question: What is Structural Induction? How is it used to prove properties of recursively defined data structures?
keyPoints: Structural Induction: Proof technique for recursively defined structures
Base Case: Prove property for the simplest/atomic elements
Inductive Step: Prove if property holds for components, it holds for the whole structure
Used for: Trees, Lists, Graphs, Expressions
Example: Binary tree - prove property for leaf, then prove if left and right subtrees have property, the whole tree does

questions.question: Explain the concept of Loop Invariant. How is it used with induction to prove algorithm correctness?
keyPoints: Loop Invariant: Condition that is true before and after each loop iteration
Three phases: Initialization (true before first iteration), Maintenance (true before→after each iteration), Termination (true when loop ends)
Used to prove partial correctness of algorithms
Example: For sorting algorithm, invariant might be: array[0..i-1] contains the i smallest elements in sorted order

questions.question: Using induction, prove that 7^n - 2^n is divisible by 5 for all n∈ℕ.
keyPoints: Base Case: n=1, 7-2=5, divisible by 5, True
Inductive Hypothesis: Assume 7^k - 2^k is divisible by 5
Rewrite as: 7(7^k - 2^k) + 5(2^k)
By hypothesis, 7(7^k - 2^k) is divisible by 5, and 5(2^k) is clearly divisible by 5
Sum of two multiples of 5 is divisible by 5, hence proven

questions.question: What is the Well-Ordering Principle? How is it logically equivalent to Mathematical Induction?
keyPoints: Well-Ordering Principle: Every non-empty set of natural numbers has a least element
Equivalent to induction: If induction fails for some P(n), consider set S of counterexamples
If S is non-empty, by WOP it has smallest element k
But k cannot be base case (proven true), and k-1 cannot be counterexample (would be smaller)
This contradiction proves induction must hold
Thus WOP ⇔ Mathematical Induction

questions.question: Prove by mathematical induction that 2^n > n² for all n ≥ 5. Also explain why this inequality does not hold for n < 5.
keyPoints: Inductive Hypothesis: Assume 2^k > k² for some k≥5
Inductive Step: Prove 2^(k+1) > (k+1)²
2^(k+1) = 2·2^k > 2·k² (by hypothesis)
Need to show: 2k² > (k+1)² = k²+2k+1
This simplifies to: k² > 2k+1, or k²-2k-1 > 0
For k≥5: 25-10-1=14>0, so holds
Fails for n=2,3,4 - explains why base must be n=5

questions.question: Using Strong Induction, prove that every integer n ≥ 2 can be expressed as a product of prime numbers.
keyPoints: Base Case: n=2, which is prime itself (product of one prime)
Inductive Hypothesis: Assume all integers from 2 to k can be written as product of primes
Inductive Step: Consider integer k+1
Case 1: If k+1 is prime, it's already a product of one prime
Case 2: If k+1 is composite, it can be written as a·b where 2≤a,b≤k
By hypothesis, a and b can be expressed as products of primes
Therefore k+1 = a·b is also a product of primes
Thus by strong induction, the theorem holds for all n≥2

questions.question: Write an algorithm to compute the sum of first n natural numbers using recursion. Prove its correctness using mathematical induction.
keyPoints: Algorithm: sum(n) = 1 if n=1, else n + sum(n-1)
Base Case: sum(1) returns 1, which equals 1²/2 + 1/2 = 1. Correct for n=1
Inductive Hypothesis: Assume sum(k) correctly computes k(k+1)/2
This equals formula for n=k+1: (k+1)((k+1)+1)/2
Hence algorithm is correct by induction
Time Complexity: O(n), Space Complexity: O(n) due to recursion stack

questions.question: Explain the difference between Weak and Strong Induction. Prove that the number of edges in a binary tree with n vertices is exactly n-1 using appropriate induction.
keyPoints: Weak: P(k)→P(k+1), Strong: P(1)∧P(2)∧...∧P(k)→P(k+1)
For binary tree, use Weak Induction on n
Base Case: n=1, tree has 0 edges, n-1=0, True
Inductive Hypothesis: Assume any binary tree with k vertices has k-1 edges
Inductive Step: Consider tree with k+1 vertices
A binary tree with >1 vertex has a root with left and/or right subtree
Let left subtree have i vertices, right have j vertices, i+j=k
By hypothesis: edges = (i-1) + (j-1) + 2 (connections to root) = i+j-1 = k-1 + 1 = k
Thus tree with k+1 vertices has (k+1)-1 = k edges
Proved by induction
